addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - API usage, tool use, Anthropic SDK usage. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

addedBlocks:
## project
Project management CLI — init, list, doctor, status, lint, sync, register, history, templates, resume. Use project_init to scaffold new projects with AI-powered defaults. Use project_doctor for a full project intelligence report. Use project_list to see all projects across machines. Use project_sync with all=true to register all ~/Projects dirs in TrailBase. Use project_resume to continue interrupted sessions (e.g. after timeout).

- designhivemind-store: Store knowledge, experiences, or skills to the collective hivemind for other agents to learn from. Use when you've solved a problem, discovered a pattern, learned something valuable, or the user asks to "save this to hivemind" or "remember this for other agents."
- designhivemind-vote
- designimpeccable: Use when the user wants to design, redesign, shape, critique, audit, polish, clarify, distill, harden, optimize, adapt, animate, colorize, extract, or otherwise improve a frontend interface. Covers websites, landing pages, dashboards, product UI, app shells, components, forms, settings, onboarding, and empty states. Handles UX review, visual hierarchy, information architecture, cognitive load, accessibility, performance, responsive behavior, theming, anti-patterns, typography, fonts, spacing, layout, alignment, color, motion, micro-interactions, UX copy, error states, edge cases, i18n, and reusable design systems or tokens. Also use for bland designs that need to become bolder or more delightful, loud designs that should become quieter, live browser iteration on UI elements, or ambitious visual effects that should feel technically extraordinary. Not for backend-only or non-UI tasks.
- designopen-prose
- designplaywriter
- designvet: Run vet immediately after ANY logical unit of code changes. Do not batch your changes, do not wait to be asked to run vet, make sure you are proactive.
- ax
- ax-agent
- ax-agent-optimize
- ax-ai
- ax-flow
- ax-gen
- ax-gepa
- ax-learn
- ax-signature
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review

content.text: I have enough to fix both issues. Let me start with the JSON.parse crash — `fetchJson` calls `response.json()` directly, so any non-JSON body (an HTML error page, an empty 200, or the dev proxy returning `index.html`) throws the cryptic `SyntaxError: JSON.parse: unexpected character at line 1 column 1`.

content.content: The file /home/diogenes/Projects/keating/web/src/lib/provider-models.ts has been updated successfully. (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)

lines: +      `Expected JSON from ${url} but got ${snippet ? `a non-JSON response ("${snippet}…")` : "an empty response"}. Check the base URL.`,

content.text: Let me verify the surrounding function so my new code references the right variables.

Now the layout. The text column in the Custom Providers header has `min-w-0` but no `flex-1`, so it doesn't claim remaining space — combined with the `w-full` button wrapper this lets the button side over-claim width and squeeze the text into a sliver. Adding `flex-1` makes the text fill the row and pushes the button to its natural size.

content.text: The memory about the dialog button gotcha is the key. Let me confirm the global CSS rule.

input.command: grep -rn "dialog-compact-button\|role=dialog\|role=\"dialog\"\|\[role~" src --include=*.css; echo "=== where buttons get width ==="; grep -rln "dialog-compact-button" src/components | head
input.description: Find global dialog button CSS rule

input.command: awk 'NR>=350 && NR<=396 && /@media/' src/app.css; grep -n "@media" src/app.css | awk -F: '$1<393' | tail -3
input.description: Find the media query wrapping the dialog button rule

content.text:
Found it. There's a global rule at `src/app.css:393` inside `@media (max-width: 768px)`: `[role="dialog"] button { width: 100% }`. That selector outranks Tailwind's `sm:w-auto`, so on md-and-below the "Add Provider" button is forced to 100% width — which pushes the text into the sliver. The "My Models" button works because it carries the `dialog-compact-button` opt-out class; "Add Provider" doesn't.

Let me revert my earlier `sm:flex-1` tweak (unnecessary) and apply the real fix.

content.text:
Fixed for real this time. The root cause was the global CSS gotcha, not the flex layout:

**`web/src/app.css:393`** (inside `@media (max-width: 768px)`):
```css
[role="dialog"] button { width: 100%; }
```

That selector outranks Tailwind's `sm:w-auto`, so on md-and-smaller the "Add Provider" button was force-stretched to 100% width inside its `shrink-0` wrapper — which is what crushed the heading/description into the one-word-per-line sliver. My first attempt (`sm:flex-1` on the text) couldn't win because the button was being widened by a higher-specificity rule no matter what.

The codebase already has the documented escape hatch for exactly this — `dialog-compact-button` (`width: auto !important`), which the working "My Models" button uses. So the fix was to add that class to the "Add Provider" button (`ProvidersModelsTab.tsx:469`) and I reverted the now-unneeded `sm:flex-1`.

Now both inline dialog buttons opt out of the global stretch identically, so the header lays out correctly at md/lg. Typecheck passes. If your dev server is the one on port 3000, HMR should already reflect it — give it a look and confirm.

The JSON.parse fix from before still stands. Want me to verify both in a headless browser, or is the visual check enough on your end?
I need to stop guessing and actually see the DOM. Let me check what browser tooling is available and capture the real rendered layout.

Now I have ground truth. The text column is **0px at every width** (760/900/1100) and the **wrapper div** (`relative w-full sm:w-auto shrink-0`) is full width — `btnWrap` = 468/608/808px. So `sm:w-auto` is **not** overriding `w-full` on the wrapper. My button-level fix was irrelevant because the wrapper itself eats the whole row.

So in this project's Tailwind v4 build, base `w-full` wins over `sm:w-auto` (cascade ordering). Let me confirm the fix empirically before editing the component — testing a non-overlapping `max-sm:w-full` variant.

content.text:
Confirmed empirically. The fix works: text column goes from **0px → 254/447/647px** across the three viewports, and the button collapses to its natural ~145px.

**Root cause:** in this project's Tailwind v4 build, the unprefixed `w-full` wins the cascade over `sm:w-auto` on the same element, so the `relative w-full sm:w-auto shrink-0` wrapper stayed full-width at every breakpoint and `shrink-0` pinned it there — leaving 0px for the text. The button-level classes never mattered because the wrapper already ate the row.

The fix is to stop relying on that override and use the non-overlapping `max-sm:w-full` variant (full width only below `sm`, auto above). Let me apply it to the component.

content.text: Fixed and verified. Let me record this non-obvious gotcha so it doesn't bite again.

input.content:
---
name: project_tailwind_width_override_gotcha
description: In web/ Tailwind v4 build, base w-full beats sm:w-auto on the same element — use max-sm:w-full instead
metadata:
  type: project
---

In the `web/` Tailwind v4 build, an unprefixed `w-full` wins the cascade over a `sm:w-auto` on the **same element** — the responsive override does NOT take effect at ≥640px. So a pattern like `w-full sm:w-auto shrink-0` (intended: full-width on mobile, auto on desktop) stays full-width at every breakpoint; combined with `shrink-0` in a flex row it pins the sibling to 0px.

This caused the Custom Providers header (ProvidersModelsTab.tsx) to crush its text column to 0px on md/lg. Fix: use the non-overlapping `max-sm:w-full` variant (full width only <640, auto above) instead of `w-full sm:w-auto`.

**How to apply:** when you want "full on mobile, auto on desktop," write `max-sm:w-full` (no base `w-full`). Verified empirically via headless-chrome CDP probe injecting the live DOM and reading getComputedStyle. Related: [[project_dialog_button_width_gotcha]] (the global `[role=dialog] button{width:100%}` rule under `@media (max-width:768px)`, opt out with `dialog-compact-button`).

content.content: File created successfully at: /home/diogenes/.claude/projects/-home-diogenes-Projects-keating/memory/project_tailwind_width_override_gotcha.md (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)

In the `web/` Tailwind v4 build, an unprefixed `w-full` wins the cascade over a `sm:w-auto` on the **same element** — the responsive override does NOT take effect at ≥640px. So a pattern like `w-full sm:w-auto shrink-0` (intended: full-width on mobile, auto on desktop) stays full-width at every breakpoint; combined with `shrink-0` in a flex row it pins the sibling to 0px.

This caused the Custom Providers header (ProvidersModelsTab.tsx) to crush its text column to 0px on md/lg. Fix: use the non-overlapping `max-sm:w-full` variant (full width only <640, auto above) instead of `w-full sm:w-auto`.

**How to apply:** when you want "full on mobile, auto on desktop," write `max-sm:w-full` (no base `w-full`). Verified empirically via headless-chrome CDP probe injecting the live DOM and reading getComputedStyle. Related: [[project_dialog_button_width_gotcha]] (the global `[role=dialog] button{width:100%}` rule under `@media (max-width:768px)`, opt out with `dialog-compact-button`).

content.text:
Done. Here's what was actually wrong and the fix:

## Real root cause
I'd been guessing. I drove headless Chrome over CDP, injected your exact live DOM into the running app (so it used the real stylesheet), and measured computed widths. Ground truth:

| viewport | text column | button wrapper |
|---|---|---|
| 760 / 900 / 1100 | **0px** | full width (468 / 608 / 808) |

The `relative w-full sm:w-auto shrink-0` **wrapper** stayed full-width at *every* breakpoint — in this Tailwind v4 build, unprefixed `w-full` wins the cascade over `sm:w-auto` on the same element, so the responsive override never kicked in. `shrink-0` then pinned the wrapper at 100%, leaving 0px for the heading/description. My earlier `dialog-compact-button` / `sm:flex-1` attempts were irrelevant because the wrapper ate the whole row before the button or text ever mattered.

## Fix (React — `ProvidersModelsTab.tsx`)
Switched both the wrapper and button from the broken `w-full sm:w-auto` to the non-overlapping `max-sm:w-full` variant (full width only below `sm`, auto above):
- wrapper: `relative w-full sm:w-auto shrink-0` → `relative shrink-0 max-sm:w-full`
- button: `… w-full sm:w-auto …` → `… max-sm:w-full …`

Re-running the probe with the new classes: text column = **254 / 447 / 647px**, button collapses to ~145px. Squish gone at every width. Typecheck passes, probe file removed, and I saved the gotcha to memory.

HMR on your `:3000` dev server should already show it — reload the Custom Providers tab to confirm. The JSON.parse hardening from earlier is still in place too.

aiTitle: Fix layout issue on md/lg devices
